data.title: Morgan Stanley: A Strong Company At A Fair Price
data.description:
Shares of Morgan Stanley (MS) have been an excellent performer over the past year, gaining 68%. The company’s focus on wealth and asset management has helped to deliver steady results, thanks to recurring revenue streams while also providing exposure to rising market levels. At the same time, elevated volatility and increased M&A have boosted its Wall Street operations. Both strengths were on display in in a strong Q1, sending shares up over 2% in early trading. Given my view many of these strengths were priced in, I cut Morgan Stanley to a “hold” in January, and shares are up modestly since then, consistent with my rating. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit MS.

In the company’s first quarter, Morgan Stanley earned $3.43, which blew past estimates by $0.41 as revenue surged 16% to $20.6 billion. MS continues to generate excellent operating leverage with pretax margins up to 34% from 32% last year. Its wealth management business again reported strong organic growth with $118 million billion of inflows, including $54 billion into its fee-based strategies. Additionally, MS set aside $98 million for credit losses, down from $135 million in Q1 of last year. Unlike commercial banks, it has limited traditional lending, and its exposure to private credit is modest, insulating results from broader credit fears.

Wealth management provides steady fee-based growth

Wealth management is the core of Morgan Stanley’s business, in my view, and its strategic shift into this area has provided more recurring fee-based revenue than Wall Street peers. Revenue was up 16% from last year to $8.5 billion. Fee-based AUM is up to $2.8 trillion, up 19% from last year. Margins were a solid 30.4% with pre-tax income coming in at $2.6 billion. Thanks to higher AUM levels, asset management fees were up 16% to $5.1 billion, and transaction fees were also strong at $1.1 billion due to elevated customer trading.

MS also generates substantial economics in this segment off of custody cash and making asset-supported loans to its high-net-worth clients. Net interest income was up to $2.17 billion, 14% higher than a year ago. For several years, client cash balances have come under pressure given higher rates in money market funds, and it appears aa bottom has been reached here. Plus, efforts to grow lending options to its clients has helped to boost yields and offset the lower fed funds rate. Separately, MS continues to manage costs well. While compensation generally grows in-line with fees, non-compensation expenses were actually down $60 million to $1.27 billion, supporting the strong margin performance in Q1.

I would note that advisory fees are charged in arrears, generally based on quarter end levels. The S&P 500 closed Q1 just over 6,500, below the end of Q4. This will be a sequential headwind for Q2 earnings, as fees are largely driven off that Q1 print. We have seen the market bounce since then, and if that holds, it will lead to a rise in Q3 revenue, all else equal. Obviously though, there is a long way to go before June 30th.

Performance fees drag on investment management

Its investment management business was actually down a modest 4% to $1.54 billion. I view this unit as likely to grow structurally slower given the ongoing pressure from passive strategies, which have limited flows and contributed to longer term pricing pressure. Indeed, AUM was up a much stronger 13%, outpacing revenue substantially, speaking to the pressure on fees. Long-term flows were positive at $3.3 billion, better than many firms struggling with outflows, but this is still quite modest.

Beyond this, the unit faced a $112 million drop in performance fees to $39 million due to weaker returns in private funds. Given some of the pressure in private markets, I would not be surprised to see performance fees across the industry run at more modest levels. Now, because of this, compensation was also down $38 million to $630 million. Absent the drop in performance fees, I would have expected a ~$20 million increase. On net, lower performance fees reduced pretax income by about ~$54 million. This more than fully explains the $43 million drop to $280 million. Essentially, modest growth ex-performance fees was overwhelmed by the drop in performance fees.

Volatility and M&A activity were further tailwinds

In its Institutional Securities business, Morgan Stanley clearly benefited from the elevated volatility after the outbreak of the war with Iran. Total revenue surged 19% from last year to $10.7 billion with pre-tax income up over 25% to $4.2 billion. Fixed income revenue was up a remarkable 90% sequentially and 29% from last year to $3.36 billion—commodities and FX trading sit inside this segment. The significant volatility in oil likely contributed to results here. Equity trading was up 25% from last year to $5.15 billion. MS is benefiting from strong client trading levels, especially in options and futures. Q2 results face a tough comparison given the remarkable volatility last year following “Liberation Day,” but the trading environment remains favorable.

Investment banking revenue also surged 36% from last year to $2.1 billion. Advisory revenue was up nearly 80% to $978 million as it receives payments from deals undertaken last year that began closing. With M&A still elevated, several major deals pending, and continued dealmaking (especially in banks as well as consumer-facing companies), I expect advisory revenue to be strong all year.

Equity underwriting rose about $80 million to $396 million, and with potential blockbuster IPOs later this year like SpaceX and OpenAI, fees should remain strong. Fixed income underwriting was also solid, up about 10% to $742 million. With the tech sector generating less free cash flow than in the past due to AI cap-ex spending, debt issuance is likely to stay structurally higher for some time, a benefit to this segment. Plus, commercial real estate activity has improved, another tailwind. As such, I expect continued growth in investment banking this year.

Capital supports buybacks; shares remain a suitable hold

Morgan Stanley maintains an excellent balance sheet with a tier 1 common equity capital position of 15.1%. This is substantially above its 11.8% regulatory minimum, enabling it to return all earnings to shareholders. I also see potential regulatory relief freeing up another $5+ of capital for returns. In Q1, it did $1.75 billion of buybacks. I now expect ~$6 billion of buybacks, up from my prior $5 billion target. Thanks to repurchases, its share count is down just over 1.5% from last year. Shares also offer a 2.2% dividend yield. I view this payout as highly secure.

Back in January, I was targeting $10.60-$11.10 in 2026 earnings. This drove my $185-190 fair value target as I believe a 17x multiple is appropriate given its fee-centric revenue. This outlook assumed a normal volatility environment, and thus far, the year has been anything but normal. As a result, I expect H1 trading activity to be substantially better. Buybacks also started the year a bit more aggressively, which will keep the average share count slightly lower. Partially offsetting this, Q2 advisory fees will be negatively impacted by 3/31 market levels and performance fees may stay muted.

Given these factors, I am now targeting $11.10-$11.60 in EPS this year. I continue to view ~17x as the appropriate multiple and am raising my price target to $195. As such, I continue to see moderate upside at current levels, making shares a suitable hold, but not enough upside to rate MS a “buy.” I would use pullbacks like we saw in March to add shares as MS’s focus on traditional wealth management leaves it fairly insulated from private credit fears as well as market volatility. Morgan Stanley proved again in Q1 the strength of its model, and it remains a strong company at a fair price.

data.title: McDonald's: On Track To 50,000 Stores And Dividend King Status, But Risks Are Mounting
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I first covered McDonald’s (MCD), I highlighted how its “Dividend King Status Nears, But Premium Valuation Meets Rising Political Risk,” rating them a Hold as their valuation didn’t seem to offer a compelling enough argument to buy yet given the macro pressure and long-term risks.

Following a solid report highlighting the company's resilience as a business despite the macro pressure, MCD’s valuation implies modest potential from current levels, with the risk-reward continuing to be unattractive while the risks stemming from the environment are rising.

On The Path to 50,000

MCD reported a solid Q4 report, beating the market’s EPS and revenue estimates, with free cash flow reaching $7.19 billion, a solid increase compared to the $6.67 billion seen in 2024 despite the significant jump in CAPEX, the latter coming as a result of the company exceeding their development plan and opening nearly 2,300 restaurants towards their target of 50,000 restaurants by the end of 2027.

This comes as MCD reported a 3.1% increase in global sales, with 2.1% comparable US sales growth, 3.2% for international operated markets (especially in Germany and Australia) and 4.6% for international developmental licensed markets, especially in Japan. The company continued its real estate investments that are ultimately key to its business model while advancing on its Accelerating the Arches strategic plan, as highlighted before, being on track to achieve the company’s fastest period of unit growth from 2024 to 2027 alongside its digital and drive thru expansion while expecting solid margins.

In a move to better compete with peers and adapt to the evolving consumer demands, McDonald’s is reportedly planning to offer new energy drinks and specialty sodas this year, which can offer a lot of potential for what are already fast-growing segments of the beverage industry while also reportedly planning to sell them at lower prices than rivals like Starbucks (SBUX) and Dutch Bros (BROS).

Although I don’t expect this to offer some major boost in sales, I still believe it highlights that their management is not doing the mistake of Nike’s (NKE) former CEO - which, as noted previously, relied on “Nike being Nike” and buybacks to deliver EPS growth, and we saw how that worked out - being willing to adapt and meet the evolving consumer needs.

Financially, based on MCD’s latest report, we continue to see a solid position, with current assets around their current liabilities and a lot of assets to cover their well-spread, low-interest debt.

The company also spent virtually everything they made in FCF on shareholder returns, spending $5.12 billion for the dividend and $2.06 billion for buybacks, for a total yield of roughly 3.32% based on today’s price, which is not that much, but it’s all they can do without using the balance sheet. The company remains a few quarters away from becoming a Dividend King, with their 50th consecutive annual dividend increase likely coming in late 2026, which will make them an official king.

McDonald’s next earnings report is expected on May 7, with the market estimating a Normalized EPS of $2.76 and $6.49 billion in revenue. I don’t expect major changes in their plans or numbers, but I will pay attention to how they aim to face the current environment, especially given the ongoing Iran conflict that can affect the global economy.

The Real Test: Near-Term Macro Pressure

Although MCD has proven its stability so far, the ongoing Iran conflict remains a major factor to pay attention to given its implications on the global economy and elevated level of risk it comes with, while the IMF warned recently that the world is already heading towards a worse scenario, which implies a 2.5% growth rate in global GDP this year.

This is even worse given the current consumer environment, as we’re talking about pressure that can go far beyond higher pump prices, with the inflationary shock that can come as a result potentially leading to higher rates or at the very least a pause in rate cuts, affecting borrowing costs while the Fed tries to fight the inflation that would limit the company’s consumers’ budgets.

This has already resulted in trade-downs and pressure that can push more and more people to avoid dining out or ordering fast-food and instead cook at home in an effort to save money, especially while retailers like Walmart (WMT) and most other majors were quick to capitalize on this phenomenon and offer their own cheaper or higher-value private label brands.

However, I highlighted before how McDonald’s was still holding strong in an environment where peers like Wendy’s (WEN) are downsizing in an effort to cut underperforming locations, which is far from what McDonald’s is doing. Recently, the company also announced their new under-$3 menu items and $4 breakfast deal, helping them further address the ongoing consumer trends through more value-oriented items.

Other than MAHA risks mentioned before and potential to see more pressure on the company to further adapt its portfolio to a more health-conscious audience in the future, there’s also the GLP-1 factor. A recent study from Morgan Stanley estimates that GLP-1 medication usage will reach about 15% of the US population by 2035, which may seem like a major point of concern, but I’d like to argue otherwise.

First of all, although I would note that we don’t have enough long-term data yet since we’re talking about a new risk factor, the impact on the spending for fast-food and limited-service restaurants dropped by about 8% for GLP-1 users - even though 54% of these users reported that they're dining out less or significantly less - which is far from an issue on a 15% adoption over a whole decade, being something the company can likely offset through pricing alone.

Secondly, and probably just as important, I just mentioned above two ways in which McDonald’s adapted to the current consumer environment and trends (i.e. energy drinks and value items), and I find it very hard to believe that this company out of everyone else would not adapt their portfolio to offer lower-calorie, high-protein, or whatever menu items would meet the new demand, especially since it’s quite easy for them to tweak their portfolio in that direction while still keeping their brand identity.

Risks Reiterated

Although we’re talking about what may as well be the best-in-class, McDonald’s is not immune to macro risks, as competition from several angles remains significant while the environment is tough to navigate.

Meanwhile, the consumer environment can also worsen significantly following the Iran conflict, which can lead to effects that not even McDonald’s can offset that well, while many restaurant peers are already struggling to some degree.

Valuation

For MCD’s DCF to Equity valuation, starting from their $7.19 billion FCF seen in 2025, I’ll assume no changes in net borrowings given their balance sheet, with an 8.5% CAGR over the first 5 years (similar to the market's expectations), backed by their expansion plan, normalizing CAPEX, margin improvements, buybacks boosting the final per share results and potential macro improvements, followed by a 5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 3% terminal growth rate given their international expansion and pricing power.

Given their strong leadership position but also macro pressure, let’s demand an 8.5% discount rate, with 714.2 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $182.86 billion, for an intrinsic value of $256.03 per share, which is still below the current $302.06 at the moment of writing this.

Conclusions

MCD remains a Hold, as the valuation seems to reflect a low potential return if everything goes according to expectations, while macro risks are piling up.

Although they are still pretty much as good as it gets when it comes to a quality business in this industry, the premium valuation makes the risk-reward level unattractive for me, while not even this company is immune to a bad enough environment.

data.title: Economic Warfare Could Lead To A Global Depression
data.description:
Economic warfare

The US-Iran war is transitioning to the next phase, and that's economic warfare. This unsuspected escalation could lead to a global depression.

Specifically, the US is now administrating a blockade of Iranian ports, with inbound and outbound traffic, and threatening other countries doing business with Iran with sanctions and secondary tariffs.

Iran is still blocking the Strait of Hormuz, which is causing a global energy supply shock.

If this economic warfare continues, a global economy will face an inflationary shock as price of crude oil spikes, and then a depression as demand collapses. Depression means a very deep and prolonged negative economic growth.

How did we get here?

The main problem is that Israel does not want a nuclear Iran - that's an existential threat to Isreal.

We need to understand what exactly the nuclear issue is with Iran.

Iran currently does not have a nuclear weapon. But there are key three terms to understand:

A threshold state

A threshold state is one that does not have a nuclear weapon, but it has all the technical capabilities to build one.

Iran is currently a threshold state: 1) enriches uranium to 60%, 2) has hundreds of kilograms of enriched uranium stockpiled, 3) operates advanced centrifuges, 4) has advanced facilities like Fordow, 5) has the scientific and industrial base, and 5) IAEA access is restricted, reducing transparency.

Breakout time

Breakout time measures the minimum time needed to produce enough weapons‑grade uranium (25 kg at 90%) for one nuclear weapon.

Current estimate Iran’s nuclear breakout time is 1–3 months under current conditions, and potentially as short as 2 weeks in a "sprint" scenario, and it was 9–12 months under JCPOA‑style constraints.

Irreversible immunity

A country has a nuclear weapon, second-strike capability, and no feasible pre-emptive strike can neutralize it. Iran does not have irreversible immunity - and this is exactly what Israel wants to prevent form happening.

Iran war Phase 1 - attempted regime change

The original mission was to decapitate Iranian leadership and force regime change, this mission failed, as regime survived despite many of its' leaders being eliminated.

Iran war Phase 2 - regional war

Iran retaliated by attacking regional energy infrastructure and the US assets in the region and closed the Strait of Hormuz to inflict economic pain to the global economy, as leverage.

Ceasefire and negotiations

The economic consequences of Strait of Hormuz closure forced a 2-week ceasefire and negotiations - to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. However, the nuclear issue prevented the deal.

The negotiations centered on extending the Iran's breakout time to 9-12 months.

Iran could possibly agree on a 5‑year moratorium: cap enrichment at ≤3.67–5%, reduce/export enriched stockpiles, freeze advanced centrifuges, restore IAEA inspections. However, Iran also asks for guarantees and sanctions relief.

However, the US wants a 20-year moratorium, with more stringent terms.

The deal is unlikely, because: 1) Iran want to preserve the threshold state status with temporary delay 2) Isreal want to take way the threshold state status completely, 3) while the US could accept a very long freeze, which would be practically permanent.

The next phase

Currently, the US and Iran are planning for a second round of negotiations before the ceasefire expires next week. These negotiations are strictly to extend the ceasefire, as the nuclear deal is unreachable.

Given that Iran has a very short-term breakout time, the situation is urgent to act.

One option for the US is to take the enriched uranium by force, and to open Hormuz with force.

Second option is to force the Iranian regime to accept the terms of negotiations by essentially starving the country and causing internal uprising - this would threaten the Iranian regime.

How do you do this? Naval blockade to prevent Iran's oil exports, and also to block Iran's imports. Also, by sanctioning all countries that try to help Iran in any way. That's economic warfare, and it's currently the preferred option.

Will it work?

Economic warfare could be an effective strategy to force Iran to the negotiating table and to accept the nuclear proposals. However, it could take a long time until Iran is ready for negotiations under duress of internal economic crisis.

During this period, Iran is likely to keep the Strait of Hormuz closed as well. Thus, the energy supply shock will worsen, in addition to the shortages of fertilizers, helium, and other key products.

Thus, the question is whether the global economy will be able to tolerate this situation.

Essentially, the US and Iran have engaged in a game of whose economy will "crack" first. Will Iran "crack" first under the blockade or will the US "crack" first under the inflationary shock.

Obviously, this game is leading to a disaster economically. The only hope for a deal is that Iran agrees to extend offered 5-year moratorium to 10 years, and US provides more guarantees, but this is still very unlikely.

Implications

So, we have the S&P500 (SP500) at the all-time highs, above the pre-war levels, while oil is 50% higher. Also note, that physical crude oil trading near $150/barrel, so even oil futures at $90 are trading based on hope.

The stock market rallied after the ceasefire was announced, rallied again after the talk were announced, rallied again after the signals for second round of talks (after the first round failed), and rallied again on the same hope.

The hope is that the deal will be made, Hormuz will reopen, oil prices will fall back to 50s - all possibly next week or within a month.

The reality is that the deal will be very hard to make, but the ceasefire is likely to be extended to allow the economic warfare strategy to work.

Economic warfare means inflation will likely accelerate as oil continues to rise and stays high - as long as Iran as able to survive the blockade, and this could be a long time.

Ultimately, this game will lead to demand collapse as countries are forced to ration energy, which means a deep prolong recession or a depression.

What's the alternative? Taking the Hormuz by force and military escalation. This is even worse scenario, as it would push oil prices much higher, and cause even higher inflation.

The US cannot declare victory and just leave - with Iran's breakout time measured in weeks.

I really don't see any other positive outcomes. The only hope is that Iran silently abandons its nuclear aspirations and gets financially rewarded - at the next round of negotiations. Later is too late, once inflation persistently spikes.

The market sees this scenario this as a baseline - and if it's wrong, we could see a sharp pullback by 10% before a deep bear market takes over.

data.title: Fortive Corporation: Growth Accelerates As Strategy Gains Traction
data.description:
Growth With Discipline

Fortive Corporation (FTV) has transformed and strengthened its strategies since my last article, which warrants a rating upgrade. Its recurring revenue base provides not only earnings visibility but also downside protection. Fluke, a part of the Intelligent Operating Solutions segment, remains a growth engine due to its exposure to the software and data center business. I also think the company’s strategy of capital allocation, including buybacks and portfolio optimization, supports earnings growth in the medium term.

But it is also important to understand the challenges. Fortive’s near-term growth may decelerate due to weak capital equipment demand. The healthcare policy flip-flop can adversely affect the AHS segment EBITDA margin. Also, demand remains uneven across regions as geopolitical risks grow. I think the company’s robust liquidity will provide the balance sheet with a sufficient cushion from any adverse shocks. It can also finance growth-related reinvestments. The relative valuation multiples have overstretched in recent months. Still, I think the stock’s long-term fundamentals have shifted well enough to call for a re-rating to a “Buy” from a previous “Hold.”

Key Drivers: Geographic and Emerging Businesses

I see three key drivers for FTV in the coming quarters. These include growth in data centers, expansion in the EMEA (Europe, the Middle East, and Africa) region, and increased sales coverage in India. Its strategy is focused on high-growth markets and regions. To capture the rapid growth in the data center business, it has introduced products like Fluke’s CertiFiber MAX (a fiber-optic testing and certification tool). This improves testing speed and accuracy for data center applications.

Fluke provides instruments, software, and services. In data centers, demand is relatively resilient across industries, including manufacturing and utilities. Multiple reports suggest strong growth in the data center business. According to this report, the global data center market is growing at ~10%–12% CAGR. Growth projection is even higher for data center infrastructure & solutions. That is why I expect the data center business to be a major growth driver.

In recent quarters, FTV’S Fluke saw solid demand, particularly from North America. Also, growth has been trending up in EMEA and Latin America, while APAC remained stable. The company’s book-to-bill exceeds 1x. The drivers emphasize the company’s resilience and forward momentum. In Q4, the company’s recurring revenues expanded at a “double-digit” pace.

Value Drivers

Let’s check out FTV’s drivers from the geography angle. In Q4, almost all regions grew, with strong performance in North America. In Europe and Latin America, the trend appears to be improving. Although gross margin deteriorated due to higher investment-related spending, its EBITDA increased by 8% in Q4. The adjusted EBITDA margin expanded following better operating leverage and cost discipline.

Although conditions have subsequently improved, demand remains sensitive and uneven. In my opinion, demand for consumables and services did not deteriorate but remained stable. One key aspect of FTV’s revenue robustness relates to recurring revenue (estimated ~50% of total sales). Software subscription is the primary pillar of recurring revenues. It has developed facility & asset lifecycle software (e.g., Accruent and ServiceChannel) and healthcare software (e.g., Provation). Because such revenues are more predictable, they typically offer margin stability and visibility. In the current state of demand volatility and uncertainty, having a strong share of a stable revenue source should attract a premium in the market.

Key Challenges

In late 2025, FTV’s AHS (Advanced Healthcare Solutions) segment ran into margin trouble. In Q4, the segment’s adjusted EBITDA margin shrank by 1.5% compared to a year earlier. In particular, capital equipment sales declined. Here, the segment sales have been volatile because they depend on transaction timing. Recent changes in healthcare policy led to cautious customer spending from Q2 through Q4. This weighed on revenue growth. I think the key challenge will continue to be the low demand for capital equipment.

Industry Factors

The ISM Manufacturing PMI increased to 53 in March compared to 52 in February. An increase in production led to this slight uptick, while lower employment and slow new orders mitigated the growth in the index in March. Because FTV’s revenues are dependent on industrial and infrastructure businesses, a PMI staying below 50 can positively affect its outlook.

My Estimates

Over the past 13 quarters, its adjusted EBITDA increased by 6% on average. Given the potential margin growth from data centers and better product mix, I expect the revenue and EBITDA to stabilize in the short term and accelerate in the medium term. Over the next four quarters, I expect its adjusted EBITDA to increase by 7%-9%.

Analyzing Q4 Performance

As disclosed in the press release published on February 4, revenues in the Intelligent Operating Solutions segment increased by 5% year-over-year in Q4. The segment’s adjusted EBITDA margin also expanded by 80 basis points in the past year. Strong performance in instrumentation, software, and gas detection led to the performance improvement. In Fluke, growth was generated in North America. Also, higher software demand and increased sales of gas detection products, particularly in the Hardware-as-a-Service part of the business, helped the operating margin in Q4.

The Advanced Healthcare Solutions segment, on the other hand, faced pressure, as I discussed above. Due to reimbursement delays and policy changes, Q4 saw lower hospital capital spending. However, late in the quarter, demand picked up from a strong pipeline of sterilization products and growth from recurring SaaS-based revenue models.

At the end of FY2025, the company’s liquidity (cash & equivalents plus available borrowings from revolving credit facility) was to $2.37 billion. But the company’s leverage (debt/equity) rose to 0.5x from 0.33 a year earlier as shareholders’ equity fell sharply (even sharper than the debt reduction). The equity fall reflects structural changes like share repurchases and the separation of Ralliant (spin-off) in June 2025. In 2H 2025, it repurchased 8% of its diluted shares outstanding. In fact, share buybacks accelerated in Q4 and continued even after the quarter ended.

Relative Valuation And Target Price

FTV appears slightly overvalued compared to its peers. Why? Because sell-side analysts expect its forward EV/EBITDA multiple to fall less steeply (versus the TTM ratio). This demands a discount over its competitors. Its EV/EBITDA ratio (~18x) is above the competitors’ (Dover Corporation (DOV), Techtronic Industries (TTNDY), and Ingersoll Rand (IR)) average. So, I think, its relative valuation is slightly overpriced with potential downside. I think the stock price’s 22% rise over the past six months stretched its valuation multiples. However, given its strong liquidity position, investors might not be uncomfortable paying a premium in the backdrop of the current geopolitical uncertainty.

As I discussed earlier in the article, I expect a 7%—9% adjusted EBITDA growth in the next four quarters. Feeding these values into the EV calculation and assuming the EV/EBITDA multiple holds, the stock can trade between $65 and $66.2, implying a ~12% upside in the near term. I think the sell-side analysts are reasonably estimating its potential (with 7% return expectations).

What Changed Since The Last Article?

I wrote my last piece on FTV in May 2025. At that time, it faced challenges from weak industry indicators, international softness, and margin & cash flow pressure. Of course, the growth drivers were present, but they were not overcoming. Since then, Fortive has pivoted toward higher-margin software with increased data center exposure. The significance of recurring revenues has rendered stability to the cash flows. The macroeconomic and industry-level drags have also subsided. That is why, despite the extended relative valuation multiples, I am re-rating it a “Buy.”

What's The Take On FTV?

FTV is increasingly placing Fluke at the center of this momentum. Data center demand and strong sales execution are the primary drivers that will keep the company growing in the mid-term. Sales-as-service (or software revenues) constitutes a sizable part of its revenues, supporting stability and margin expansion. I think improving leverage and disciplined cost control will complement its efforts in the long term.

However, weakness in capital equipment demand and the current healthcare policy point to uncertainty. This caused the AHS segment operating margin to decline in Q4. I expect this to weigh on near-term growth. But medium-term growth should improve as strategic initiatives gain traction. The company has robust liquidity. I understand that the slightly rich multiples compared to its peers are not ideal from a valuation perspective. But I think the positive factors will outweigh the negatives. So, I recommend investors “Buy” the stock.

data.title: What In The World? 3 Country ETFs That Look Good Vs. S&P 500
data.description:
This Country ETF performance table from Seeking Alpha is a handy quick reference. I'll be talking about some of the ETFs on this list, and I suggest tracking it going forward.

Because while the US stock market continues to show resilience at the headline level, there's something I see that will increasingly be on my radar. In fact, it continues to creep up more and more every month. It has to do with the scores of ETFs on that list, each devoted to a different single country's stock market.

These ETFs have been around for a while. But I have believed for some time that investors overlook them. That's too bad, since there's a particular use of them which, if more investors thought about it, could be a real portfolio gap-filler for them.

What do I see that many do not? That these country ETFs are much more than a convenient way to "go international" in an otherwise US-oriented portfolio. They might sound exotic, or have the allure of tapping into emerging markets, as many do. But that's not what I see.

To me, the missing link with these ETFs is more basic, and hiding in plain sight. In a US market that is dominated by technology stocks, and big tech in particular, the wide array of single-nation ETFs represent sector diversification. And industry diversification.

And market cap size diversification too. And as a technician to the core, I am obligated to tell you this as well: they often present differently, chart-wise.

Then there's the traditional use of these ETFs, as part of the non-US equity allocation concept. Since they own stocks traded on their local markets, when the currencies of those countries appreciate against the US Dollar, they get what is akin to that old joke about the SAT test for US high school students: you get 400 points just for writing your name! Because regardless of how the stocks do in local currency, when translated to US Dollars to price the ETF, there's often a lift in value.

Free money? Not exactly, But sort of like that

Diversification is the proverbial "free lunch" on Wall Street. But the dirty little secret, again hiding in plain sight for investors, is the uniform nature with which so much of the US stock market trades.

To me, that is THE portfolio construction issue of our time. And since this phase of my career is highly-focused on teaching investors how to be their own portfolio manager, this tendency of investors to still think the market is filled with stocks moving out of sync with each other is simply not accurate. And likely a product of Wall Street marketing muscle.

Look at the chart above. Which one of those looks good to you? Which is your favorite? Hard to tell, right? That's because while those five market segments should offer some degree of diversification, when we smooth the data over 6 -month rolling periods, there's not much space between them. That's the uniform market returns thing playing out, right in front of our eyes. And it leaves me looking for something, anything, that offers a change of pace.

Increasingly, I'm looking outside the US. But the broad-based, "default" international ETF types are, to me, nothing special. Here they are, with the average S&P 500 stock (RSP). There's some daylight, but this looks more like a beta situation to me. That is, international developed (EFA) and emerging markets (EEM) typically rise and fall more than RSP does. That's not high-value diversification. They are still too correlated.

Now, open up your mind and consider the potential of scouting a list of ETFs that includes these. Data on portfolio baskets within country funds is difficult to assess. But my point in showing this to you is the array of P/E Ratios. There are country ETFs for nations as off the financial radar as Kuwait, Belgium and Peru. So to me, this is like a different brand of small cap, value or non-big cap tech exposure. And I like it!

I've picked out a few from among these many options for investors. To me, investing is a process of rotation, so there are no "long-term buys" here. The idea for me is to track as many country ETFs as I can, and use my technical work to see which ones possess two things at any point in time.

First, a good chart. And second, an ETF that convinces me that I am not just buying a fund that tracks the US major stocks market indexes up and down, with market-level beta or at least a consistent beta in both directions. Because as I established above, that is not adding a darn thing to my total portfolio. And that can lead to a malady I call "a collection of securities."

I'd rather have a true, smartly-diversified portfolio. Here are a few that look good to me on a tactical basis currently. As it turns out, I've owned each of these at one point in the past, among the 20-25 of these I've owned over time.

iShares MSCI Chile ETF (ECH)

As with many single country ETFs, Chile is often viewed by outsiders as a play on a particular niche industry with that nation. In this case, it is the country's mining sector. Chile is among the world's leading producers of lithium and copper.

That grabs my attention, since the S&P 500 includes the Basic Materials sector, but only barely. It is typically 2-3% of that index. Here, between miners and banks, 45% of ECH is accounted for.

I can see a path to a 10% gain from here, perhaps more, before the recent all-time high comes into play. Again, this is a matter of lining up a few dozen of these ETFs, then doing a portfolio manager's job, of rotating among the chess pieces. ECH makes my list.

iShares MSCI Denmark ETF (EDEN)

Denmark is best known these days as the country that owns Greenland. But the Danish stock market is tilted toward pharmaceutical stocks, as well as those in renewable energy. A major pharma stock, Novo Nordisk (NVO), sits atop EDEN, at an 18% position.

iShares MSCI Indonesia ETF (EIDO)

Indonesia makes a good case for being on my radar, as its economy is expected to grow by more than 5% this year. That actually disappointed versus forecasts, but that's part of why EIDO is trading around where it did in 2011. Yes, value investing matters outside the US too. Bank stocks dominate here, with financials at nearly 40% of assets.

And do you see the tech sector exposure? No... you don't! There is none. This is what I mean: I can get all the technology giants I want by owning SPY or QQQ. But if I really want to diversify in a meaningful way, single country ETFs are a great consideration. ECH also has zero tech weighting, while EDEN has just 1%.

Finally, I checked these on my ROAR Score risk management system. As expected the better charts (ECH and EDEN) sport green zone (lower risk) grades, at 70 and 60 respectively. Most ETFs top out at 80, and 30 and lower is considered higher risk.

And that's why I'm presenting EIDO here. Because while its ROAR Score is only at 30, that chart has the look of an ETF that has a fighting chance to climb out of its recent basement situation. ROAR identified the high risk here in early February, prior to a 12% dip. But with market conditions a bit more washed out for now, this one makes my list. ROAR Scores of 10 and 20 are typically where bottom-fishing expeditions start, and EIDO recently took the first step, increasing from 20 to 30 this week.

This just scratches the surface on this topic. But I plan to keep this front of mind going forward. I'm even building a country ETF rotation portfolio for my website, and I'll report its progress here from time to time.

data.title: Why Bullish Is Positioned To Capture The Institutional Crypto Wave
data.description:
Bullish (BLSH) now offers an attractive entry point for long-term investors in a weak crypto market. This exchange differentiates itself by targeting institutions and appealing to them with a focus on compliance and a wide product range.

Bullish owns everything from a crypto media business to a clearinghouse. As institutions move more money to crypto, there is room for expansion of the business they do with Bullish.

Before gaining approval in the US this past October, Bullish was international. This started with markets such as Gibraltar, Hong Kong, and Singapore, where daily trading volumes reached ~$2 billion in Q2. This gave them the credibility and platform to operate in the US. Since then, they have launched options and become the third-largest centralized crypto exchange by spot volume.

With institutions looking to allocate more to digital assets, Bullish is a top contender for that money since it targets compliance and differentiated products. While the crypto market has cooled off, it offers an attractive risk/reward opportunity to invest in a fast-growth crypto exchange. That's why I'm rating Bullish a Buy with a 12-month price target of $47.97 per share.

Management and Notable Investors

Tom Farley, the CEO, sets Bullish apart from other exchanges through his background as a former president of the New York Stock Exchange. This translates to how Bullish does business with a focus on compliance and risk management. Farley previously led Far Peak Acquisition Corp. and Far Point Acquisition Corp., which were both SPACs focused on market infrastructure. The co-founder of Bullish, Brendan Blumer, is the CEO of Block.one, the technology company behind EOS blockchain, and holds 30% of Bullish's shares. Peter Thiel is another early-stage backer of the company.

Given how much institutions care about compliance and regulation, Bullish has a huge advantage with an ex-NYSE president on its side. This should give them an edge when fighting for institutional customers, given his reputation and relationships.

Institutional Adoption

With regulations becoming clearer, institutions are starting to move into digital assets. According to an EY-Parthenon and Coinbase Institutional Digital Assets Survey, 73% of institutional investors plan to increase digital asset allocation in 2026. Another shift is the number one risk for institutions, which used to be volatility, but is now security and regulatory compliance. This is something Bullish has put focus on since day one.

Regulations across the globe are also becoming clearer, with the GENIUS Act signed into law in July 2025. This provides strict oversight and regulation, allowing stablecoins to be a real part of financial ecosystems. Project Crypto, led by the SEC and CFTC, has also been launched to provide classification for all types of digital assets. The CLARITY Act is also in its final stages, which, once passed, will specify a framework allowing access to all types of institutional money. Europe has also taken steps to regulate crypto with the MiCA regulation entering its final step. By July 2026, all providers must be authorized by the regulation, clarifying classifications for all EU businesses.

With the digital asset basics finally being regulated, institutional focus has started to turn to tokenization. The EY-led study also showed that 67% of institutions are now looking at tokenization capabilities over the next two years. BlackRock CEO Larry Fink also mentioned this in his recent annual letter, saying that digital asset projects could become a major revenue driver for BlackRock within five years.

Technology

Bullish's technology is a combination of a traditional Central Limit Order Book (CLOB) with an automated market maker (AMM). By taking both systems, liquidity is improved. A lot of centralized exchanges like Coinbase, Binance, and Kraken use CLOB systems, while most DeFi protocols use AMMs. Bullish is one of the few that uses both, and it seems the strategy is working given the growth of volume.

Volume Growth

Bullish's volume has continued to increase, leading them to pass Coinbase in February to become the third in spot volume for centralized crypto exchanges. The $76 billion in volume is the highest since October of last year, when crypto prices were higher. Bullish's volumes have continued to grow, even in this market when other exchange volumes are falling. This suggests the company's focus on institutions is working.

Options volume has helped Bullish grow, with a volume of $9 billion and peak open interest of $4 billion at the end of January. Not only is this higher-margin revenue, but it also aligns with Bullish's institutional focus, where traders use options for more complex trades. For 2026, management is looking to grow the derivatives market and continue to build a strong US presence. If they are able to secure a US derivatives license, it opens up the largest regulated crypto derivatives market in the world.

Treasury

Although it operates as an exchange, Bullish holds a large amount of crypto on its balance sheet for liquidity. This ultimately affects its valuation, making the stock closely tied to crypto prices. As of the most recent release, the company holds ~$3.2 billion of digital assets on its balance sheet. With the 15-20% downturn in major cryptocurrencies since the start of the year, the amount is likely closer to $2.4-2.6 billion. At a market cap of ~$6.1 billion, the digital assets provide a significant amount of the company's value. This prices the exchange at roughly ~$3.6 billion (after subtracting net debt to get treasury NAV), which I believe is undervalued.

Recent Results

The company's FY2025 adjusted revenue reached $288.5 million, with $92.5 million of that coming in Q4. Adjusted EBITDA for Q4 came in at $44.5 million, giving a baseline of $178 million in adjusted EBITDA and $370 million for revenue. This makes the Q4 adjusted EBITDA margin 48.1%, showing continued improvement. Adjusted net income hit a new high of $28.9 million, up significantly from $4 million a year ago.

A lot of future growth is expected in the subscription, services, and other segment (SS&O), with management targeting 50% growth in 2026. This is expected to be led by its options trading platform, which accounted for nearly 29% of global Bitcoin options volume within months of its launch. Through its subsidiary CoinDesk, Bullish has become the #1 indexer by market share for digital asset ETFs, and won 15 of 39 global digital asset ETF listings in Q4. With institutional demand growing, Bullish is well positioned to take advantage of it.

Valuation

Bullish looks undervalued relative to other companies in the industry. Coinbase trades at roughly 6.6x forward revenue, while Bullish trades at closer to ~14.5x forward revenue. Although Bullish's multiple is higher, I believe it undervalues the growth potential and expectations. I believe a re-rating is likely considering the 50% expected growth in SS&O and institutional tailwinds.

Accounting for a treasury net asset value of ~$2.6 billion and a forward revenue multiple of 12x on estimates of $383 million, my 12-month price target is $47.97 per share after dividing by shares outstanding of around 150 million. This could be heavily impacted by crypto prices, which could either boost value with increased demand and a more valuable treasury or decrease value with less demand and lower treasury value in a weaker market. Given the current and expected tailwinds, I believe Bullish will see more demand and a more valuable treasury.

Risks to the Thesis

The crypto market is extremely volatile and poses risks to Bullish's valuation. Any further declines in crypto could result in negative near-term results.

Regulations are still not fully clear on crypto. Although bills are in the works, any delay could have a negative impact on institutional demand.

Competition is intense, with players like Coinbase, Binance, and Kraken being in the market for years. Although this is the case, Bullish has continually proven itself and increased its market share. I expect this to continue given the company's differentiated approach.

Takeaway

Through credible leadership and a wide range of products, Bullish is positioned to be a top crypto exchange. With a weakened crypto market, there is a good entry point for investors as Bullish enters a year of strong growth.

As regulations become clearer, institutions are gearing up to allocate more money towards digital assets. With a strong focus on transparency and a wide product range, Bullish is set up to receive some of that business. Since crypto headwinds are pushing down related exchange valuations, it provides an opportunity to buy the dip on a company like Bullish. That's why I'm rating Bullish a Buy with a 12-month price target of $47.97 per share.

data.title: Bank of America: Q1 Earnings Keep The Bullish Thesis Intact
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier this morning, Bank of America Corporation (BAC) reported better than expected earnings for the first quarter of 2026. The large bank’s shares were up in premarket trading. I’ve been a fan of both the bank’s common and preferred shares, with my last analysis coming in February. While risks related to the Iran conflict and private credit weigh on the markets, I believe that Bank of America is well positioned to generate stable shareholder returns.

Bank of America Q1 Earnings

Lower interest rates have benefited Bank of America and allowed the bank to grow its margins. In the first quarter, asset yields dropped by 12 basis points to 4.38%. Fortunately, borrowing yields saw a steeper decline of 17 basis points to 3.00%. As rates have declined over the last couple of years, Bank of America has managed to maintain its asset yield decline at a lower rate than borrowing yields.

These changes are best displayed by examining the net interest spread, which has steadily grown over the last couple of years and rose by 5 basis points in the first quarter to 1.38%. When incorporating leverage, the net interest margin is also improving, although at less consistency. In the first quarter, the net interest margin did decline by 1 basis point, but at 2.07%, it is at the second highest level since the first quarter of 2023.

These changes in rates and rate management are best exemplified in looking at interest income and expense. In the first quarter, Bank of America experienced an equidistant decline in both metrics of approximately $900 million. This led to net interest income (interest income less interest expense) to be flat on a quarterly basis at around $15.75 billion. Despite the flat quarterly change, net interest income’s trend continues to be positive for the bank.

Changes in Loans and Deposits

As interest rates have declined, Bank of America has maintained impressive and consistent loan growth. Loans grew at 1.7% in the first quarter, marking seven consecutive quarters of loan growth of over 1%. Because of the consistency in loan growth, the year-over-year metrics are even more impressive, with loans growing at 8.6% over the last year.

On the deposit side, growth has been less impressive, but still acceptable. Bank of America grew deposits by 0.9% in the first quarter, representing the third quarter of deposit growth in the last four. Deposit growth has come in under loan growth for several quarters in a row, yet the bank has managed to grow deposits at a respectable 2.4% over the last year.

The trend of loan growth over deposit growth has caused Bank of America’s loan to deposit ratio to edge higher over the last four years. Fortunately, the bank has managed a very conservative loan to deposit ratio, so having it grow to 58% is not particularly alarming. The average commercial bank in the U.S. has a higher loan to deposit ratio, so Bank of America has room to grow its loan book through the financing of deposits and thus keep its net interest income under control.

Bank of America does have an investment banking arm that leads to a large degree of external financing. The bank’s external financing edged higher to nearly $737 billion in the first quarter. However, in comparison to the size of the bank’s loan balance, the levels of external financing have been consistent for the last few years.

Risks to Bank of America

While I have been harping on loan performance as the primary risk of banks for several months, I believe higher short-term interest rates are the largest current risk to Bank of America. With the conflict in Iran bringing energy price shocks, higher inflation could bring higher short-term interest rates with it. If that happens, Bank of America will see its yields compress and its net interest income come under pressure. Investors should specifically watch the spread between short-term and long-term interest rates as banks borrow in the short-term and lend over longer periods of time.

Loan performance also remains a concern. Unlike many of the other big banks, Bank of America carries a lower allowance for credit losses ratio at 1.09% of gross loans. Fortunately, the bank has had stable loan performance with the percentage of nonperforming loans being under 50 basis points of gross loans for the second time in two years during the first quarter. It’s not to say that headwinds in loan performance, especially if combined with higher short-term rates, can’t create volatility for shareholders, so that needs to be monitored continuously.

Conclusion

Bank of America continues to deliver better than expected results for shareholders despite the headwinds facing the economy. The bank is doing a great job of increasing its loans and managing loan performance. I am confident that without an unexpected rise in short-term rates, the bank will continue to deliver healthy results for investors.

data.title: Mosaic: This Sell-Off Is A Classic Contrarian Opportunity
data.description:
I Rate MOS As A "Buy"

The Mosaic Company, Inc. (MOS) is an American $7.6-billion market cap producer of mineral fertilizers that focuses specifically on concentrated phosphate and potash crop nutrients. For the past few months, the MOS stock has been dropping like a rock because of a few reasons that I'll discuss later in the article.

But from what I see, the correction might have gone too far with some clear bullish catalysts on the horizon - once they kick in, MOS should rebound from today's price levels. I don't expect we'll see matching with CF Industries' (CF) returns, but still, I believe MOS is a "Buy" following its latest meltdown.

Why Do I Think So?

Let's understand why MOS has been underperforming CF and some other peers in the fertilizer sphere over the past few quarters. In February 2026, MOS received a downgrade from JPMorgan analysts, who explained why 2026 would be an ugly year for the stock - and so far, their prediction has been playing out well.

Mosaic is likely to experience a year of lower earnings in 2026 as a base case. Mosaic’s business portfolio has two large divisions: a phosphate operation and a potash business. Phosphates use sulfur and ammonia as key raw materials. Phosphate prices are moving somewhat lower because of affordability issues, while sulfur prices have about doubled, and ammonia has risen as well. Mosaic buys about 2.2 million tons of sulfur annually, and we estimate that its average sulfur costs will rise by about $525 million in 2026 year over year, from $469 million to $994 million, as its per-ton costs increase from $236/ton to $460/ton. The difficulties in the phosphate market have led to higher-cost operations in South America being shuttered because of high sulfur prices and poor grower incomes. Mosaic’s Fertilizantes operation could move lower in EBITDA from $567 million to $235 million in 2026. There is likely to be negative earnings momentum as a base case in the first and second quarters of 2026, both sequentially and year over year, as these inflationary costs work their way into the Mosaic income statement. Our 2026 EBITDA estimate for Mosaic is $1.96 billion versus $2.4 billion in 2025.

Source: JP Morgan's note on MOS [February 2026]

So the main reason why the Mosaic stock sold off was the rise in sulfur prices because of the war in the Middle East. The firm uses this mineral to produce its final selling products, and given the fact that 44% of the world's seaborne sulfur exports flow directly through the Strait of Hormuz, MOS's forward-looking costs started to look significant. The above JPM call was made in late February, and according to Tradingeconomics data, the sulfur prices have skyrocketed further from ~$3810 to ~$6483 CNY/ton.

In addition to that, the latest Q4 update from Mosaic looked like an anomaly - because of a sudden collapse in U.S. fall application demand (-20% YoY), the firm burned a lot of cash, also showing Q4 net loss of $519 million (a GAAP EPS loss of $1.64), heavily missing the bottom line consensus estimate for the quarter. The above-described tendency on the sulfur market, as well as the looming margin contraction prospects ahead, have created a setup for further negative earnings revisions, which have gotten worse over the past month:

I believe that all this is a temporary pressure for MOS, and the market is very likely to revise the longer-term earnings expectations as the geopolitical setup eases.

The reopening of the Strait of Hormuz is a crucial factor here. According to The Hindu's data, a U.S.-sanctioned Chinese tanker has successfully navigated the Strait of Hormuz recently, despite the ongoing tensions and the informal blockade in the region. We know that China's export restrictions that were imposed lately on some minerals apply to phosphate fertilizers, not sulfur, so the end users of sulfur will be able to get the much-needed resources. And since China restricts phosphate fertilizer exports now, MOS should benefit from it, although its COGS will be much higher than usual. According to Tradingeconomics, Di-ammonium prices are also on the rise now, so they'll logically offset the rise in COGS to some extent.

Mosaic was already seeing inquiries from international buyers who traditionally relied on Chinese exports as of February 2026, according to the management's commentaries on the Q4 call, and I guess that after the new Chinese restriction now, and the whole situation with the Strait, the amount of such inquiries is skyrocketing.

If [China's restriction] goes beyond the first half of the year, there may be even more opportunities on the international side. But we are getting reach out from customers who traditionally may be served more by the Chinese export market looking for tonnes. And I think it's important to understand that from our view anyways that the phosphate market is a supply-constrained market.

Source: MOS's Q4 earnings call, notes added

The firm guided for over 7 million tonnes of phosphate production for FY2026, and if they deliver on that, I think fixed costs will be absorbed - every 100,000 tonnes of increased quarterly production lowers conversion costs by $7-8/t, based on management commentary. So, despite the rise in sulfur-related costs, driving conversion costs below $100 per tonne for Mosaic might be well within reach for this year.

The market has already reflected the upcoming EBITDA headwind of $250 million for Q1 2026 because of the sulfur pricing, but the volumes ramp-up looks like a clear buffer that can help MOS save on the margins side.

And then in addition to how we're thinking about that is that the more we push on getting to our full capacity on phosphate fertilizer production, it's just going to continue to expand with that fixed cost absorption, our margin to insulate us even more from some of the uncontrollables on the raw material side. So farmer affordability, I think, is going to cover it. How much we can pass-through, there probably is a limit. But we've got still a lot of room to go before we feel a lot of pressure on margin -- stripping margin standpoint for profitability around phosphate.

Source: MOS's Q4 earnings call, emphasis added

The FY2026 EPS drop that is priced in at -26.82%, per consensus data, should be followed by a massive rebound in FY2027 (+46.72% YoY expected), which I believe may be underestimated now as the analysts likely fail to take into account the fixed-cost absorption thesis that I have in mind.

The discrepancy between the lowest EPS estimate for FY2027 at $0.90 and the highest at $3.74 is quite large, and I think the latter is closer to being true. If the demand side remains strong over the next few years - and I think it will - and Mosaic's margins won't drop as much as it's currently expected by the market, the firm might close FY2027 with $3/share in adjusted earnings. Trading at 10-12x, it's going to give Mosaic a fair price per share at $33, which is about 37% above today's stock price. Wall Street analysts are almost as bullish as I am, based on Seeking Alpha's consensus price target data:

That's why I'm rating MOS as a "Buy" today, although the meltdown continues.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

The biggest risk to my thesis today is that sulfur prices rise higher and the cost of goods sold on Mosaic's income statement increases to much higher levels than I'm anticipating. Another bearish factor that might emerge after August 2026 is if China decides to lift its current export restrictions and Chinese manufacturers start to flood global markets, hence eating away at MOS's market share.

There's also a near-term pressure when it comes to Mosaic's FCF generation. The firm burned $535 million in free cash flow in FY2025, and while the management team promised a massive working capital release, any delays in farmer demand due to weather or pricing standoffs could keep that cash trapped in inventory. Plus, we need to keep in mind that MOS requires $1.5 billion in CAPEX in 2026 to address gyp stacks and tailings dams, so we'll likely see some continued pressure on the cash flow statement in the next quarters.

My technical analysis shows that if MOS breaks down its current support level, it can head to the 2020 lows in the coming quarters - the downside might be massive in this case:

The Bottom Line

While I recognize the existence of risks for Mosaic to date, I'm leaning toward the contrarian side. The stock seems to be trading at a deep discount right now, and when we look forward to 2026-2027, we might see the stage for a massive earnings recovery. That's why I'm rating MOS as a Buy.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Nutanix: The Great Exodus From VMware And, Possibly, An Explosion Of Operating Leverage
data.description:
The market often punishes companies for necessary transformations, perceiving temporary operational pain as a terminal stage of the decline of business. For many years, Nutanix (NASDAQ: NTNX) found itself exactly in such a trap. Right now the shares trade in the area of $37, which gives, at first glance, a fair forward P/E on the level of 21. At the same time, there are grounds to suppose that a certain inaccuracy is laid into current valuations. The market, possibly, prices the shares as if the recent exit into net profit is a linear, one-time anomaly. In reality, though, I suppose that Nutanix is located at an inflection point: conditions are forming, perhaps, for the launch of a colossal operating leverage, provoked by a historical shift in the market of IT infrastructure. Probably, this is not the end of a growing cycle but the very start of a large-scale migration of the corporate sector.

The Profile & The Problem

To realize the scale of opportunities of Nutanix, it is needed to look "under the hood" of a modern data center. Traditionally, IT infrastructure was built as a siloed infrastructure from three disparate levels: servers for computations, massive systems of data storage, and network equipment tying them together.

This three-level architecture is, most likely, a real nightmare for business. It requires the purchase of expensive "hardware" from different vendors, an army of narrow-profile engineers for setup, and huge areas in server rooms. If a company needs more power, it cannot simply press a button—it is necessary to buy additional new server racks, which takes months.

Nutanix made a peculiar revolution, having become the pioneer of hyper-converged infrastructure. They created "smart" software, which factually replaces all these hardware racks. Instead of a complex hierarchy, a company can buy several standard, inexpensive servers, and the software from Nutanix will unite them into a single, flexible "cloud". This is like the transition from huge computing centers of the 70s to a modern smartphone: everything that earlier occupied rooms and required complex commutation now works inside a single software environment.

But here is a nuance: on the path to success, the company experienced significant growing pains. In the period from 2018 to 2022, Nutanix took a strategic decision: practically fully to pivot away from hardware sales and become a pure software company with a subscription model (SaaS).

For investors, this became a shock. A technical failure occurred in the accounting: instead of receiving millions of dollars for sold equipment right away (CapEx), the company began to receive small monthly payments for subscription (OpEx). On paper, revenue stagnated, and losses frightened the market. Wall Street wrote Nutanix off, having considered them outsiders, probably not capable of competing with cloud giants. And here we have the result: exactly in this moment that undervaluation was born, which we see today.

The error of the system

While Nutanix agonizingly rebuilt its financial model, in the market of virtualization (a technology that allows one server to work like ten independent machines), seemingly, VMware undividedly reigned. It was the standard of the industry, the "engine" on which worked the software of almost every large corporation in the world.

The situation fundamentally changed after the giant Broadcom swallowed VMware. The leadership of Broadcom committed what I call a "systemic mistake of domination". They decided to radically monetize their domination, applying an aggressive extraction strategy. Firstly, a segmentation prioritizing top-tier enterprises occurred: Broadcom openly stated that exclusively the 2000 largest world clients (Global 2000) interest them. The rest of the market was factually thrown to the mercy of fate. Secondly, we see a price shock: for medium and large businesses, prices for licenses skyrocketed by 300–500%. Broadcom forcibly killed perpetual licenses, having forced clients to transition to extremely expensive package offers (bundles) into which unneeded functions are included. Thirdly, the destruction of the ecosystem began. Thousands of partners and distributors of VMware around the whole world suddenly discovered that the conditions of cooperation became, for them, perhaps, onerous.

Nature abhors a vacuum. By its own hands, Broadcom created an artificial vacuum, having pushed out from its ecosystem thousands of clients who are now looking for a "safe haven". In 2026, a clear picture is emerging: one of the real alternatives, capable of accepting this flow of refugees without loss of quality, probably appears to be Nutanix. The key advantage of Nutanix here is its own free hypervisor, AHV. For companies, it is no longer necessary to pay the "tax on virtualization" of Broadcom; they can simply transfer their systems onto Nutanix and receive that same reliability at a fraction of the cost. I suppose that this process of migration is only gaining momentum, and the numbers in the last reports are possibly only the tip of the iceberg.

Financial retro-analysis

Historical data clearly reflect the trauma of the SaaS transformation. Revenue grew agonizingly slowly: from $1.31 billion in 2020 to $1.39 billion in 2021 and $1.58 billion in 2022, at that time as the operational margin remained deeply negative.

At the same time, the free fall stopped. By 2024, revenue reached $2.15 billion, and the net loss shrank. In 2025 the business model seemingly eliminated pass-through costs: revenue broke through $2.60 billion, and the company generated $267 million of net profit. The illusion of stagnation has dispelled itself; the bottom is probably passed, and the subscription engine is earning at full power.

Investment idea: Turnaround

The core of our bullish thesis is the acceleration of the flight of clients from Broadcom, which we visually see in the freshest reports. Decisions about the change of IT infrastructure are not taken in a week. All through 2024 and 2025, CIOs of corporations planned budgets, and only right now do we see the actual shift in spending.

In the quarter concluded in January 2026, Nutanix showed explosive growth: revenue flew up to $722.8 million (against $670.3 million a quarter earlier). More importantly, net profit jumped to $103.5 million for one quarter (compared with a modest 30-60 million in previous periods). The free cash flow for this same period constituted an impressive $191 million.

More than that, at the last conferences and by the valuations of analysts, the company recorded a record influx—about 1000 new clients (logos) per quarter, which appears to be the best indicator over the last 8 years. This is factual proof that the turnaround transitioned into an active phase.

This turnaround bases itself on three fundamental factors. Firstly, the capture of a growing market is going on. The entire HCI market is valued at approximately $19.5-20 billion with a pace of growth around 17%. The share of VMware (~40%) right now rapidly melts, and Nutanix aggressively takes away this volume (~30% and growing). Secondly, the factor of GPT-in-a-Box 2.0 and Agentic AI works. Corporations want to implement AI but are afraid to give confidential data into public clouds (OpenAI/Azure). Nutanix offers a ready On-Premise solution for the safe launch of AI inside the perimeter of the company. Literally in April 2026 at the conference .NEXT, the company announced the platform Nutanix Agentic AI in partnership with NVIDIA and also an expanded partnership with AMD. The appearance of NKP Metal for work with Kubernetes makes them not simply infrastructure, but, probably, a full-fledged foundation for the launch of corporate AI. Thirdly, the Trojan horse from Dell played its role. Dell, for a long time, was a key partner of VMware. At the same time right now, to not lose infuriated clients, Dell actively sells its servers, PowerEdge, with pre-installed software of Nutanix. The competitor turned into a most powerful channel of distribution.

Valuation

A possible optical inaccuracy of the current valuation is the main reason for an attentive study of the asset. Let's look at the numbers: if we take the last quarterly profit ($103.5 million) and simply linearly extrapolate it onto four quarters, we will receive about $414 million of yearly profit. At the current capitalization of approximately $10.5 billion, this gives a P/E in the area of 25.

The fact that the forward P/E is valued by analysts at 21x speaks to the fact that the market lays down an extremely modest, practically linear growth. For the growing technological sector of the USA, a capitalization of $10 billion at a revenue of $2.6+ billion (multiplier P/S around 3.5-4.0) is highly conservative. For comparison, overheated cloud services trade with P/S from 10 to 15.

It seems the current valuations do not in full measure account for the mathematics of operating leverage. Nutanix's expenses on development and marketing have already stabilized. Every new subscription from former clients of VMware flows practically straight to the bottom line. It turns out that if revenue growth continues and the additional margin transforms itself into profit at current paces, the real indicators can significantly surpass linear expectations. Furthermore, the Seeking Alpha Quant Rating system currently supports this constructive outlook, reflecting strong underlying momentum.

The most important catalyst and confirmation of the undervaluation is the fresh news from 7 April 2026: the board of directors officially approved an increase of the program of buyback of shares by $750 million. Companies rarely allocate such sums on buybacks if management is not confident in the long-term stability of its free cash flow.

As soon as the market realizes that Nutanix is not old, clumsy software but a growing beneficiary of AI infrastructure, a revaluation of multipliers can occur. If they begin to value the company as a growing business, multipliers can expand, which will give the shares an additional impulse.

Risks

A turnaround of such scale, probably, does not happen smoothly. The main macroeconomic risk is the harsh policy of the Fed. A lengthy preservation of high interest rates makes crediting expensive, which can force corporations to tighten their belts and postpone large-scale programs of updating servers. Besides that, despite the current benefit for Dell from the sales of the software of Nutanix, Dell has its own developments. If they decide to aggressively promote proprietary solutions, Nutanix can lose a part of partner channels of sales.

Conclusion

I suppose that Nutanix trades with a noticeable discount to its potential efficiency. The valuation of the market, possibly, is still clouded by the heavy SaaS transformation of past years and conservatively values the potential of the operating leverage, having manifested itself in the beginning of 2026. The free cash flow confidently grows, the program of share repurchases is expanded, and the policy of Broadcom continues to stimulate the transition of clients into the ecosystem of Nutanix. At current multipliers (P/S ~4, Forward P/E ~21) and the most powerful technological foundation for the implementation of corporate AI, the shares of NTNX, in our view, have a significant margin of safety. Probably, right now an attractive moment is forming itself for a long-term entry, while the models of valuation were not revised in favor of a more aggressive growth.

data.title: Nike: An Upcoming Dividend Aristocrat Trading At A 12-Year Low
data.description:
A Story Surrounded By Pessimism

In today's article, I am looking at another beaten-down company in the name of Nike (NKE), the largest seller of athletic footwear and apparel in the world. This comes after my previous research on CoStar (CSGP), Copart (CPRT) and Novo Nordisk (NVO) so far this month. All of those companies are similar in the sense that they have all been facing a challenging operating environment over the last few years, that is despite being clear market leaders in their respective markets. What is also interesting to mention is that every one of those four companies was at one point a market darling trading at lofty valuation multiples, which highlights the cyclical nature of things on financial markets. As the famous trader of the early 20th century William Delbert Gann wrote in his book 'The Law of Vibration':

Everything moves in cycles as a result of the natural law of action and reaction. By a study of the past, I have discovered what cycles repeat in the future.

In a way, the entrenched pessimism surrounding Nike is perfectly understandable, as the stock is currently trading at a price level last seen in October 2014, or almost 12 years ago. This is indeed a long time for an investment to sit idle, especially considering the significant volatility that occurred in between. Moreover, this price performance does not take into account the significant increase in the money supply that has occurred since October 2014, with the national debt of the United States more than doubling, from $18 trillion at the end of 2014 to $39 trillion as of early April 2026. In real terms, Nike's share price performance has been nothing short of disastrous, although the company's reliable dividend payments may have eased some pain for long-term shareholders. On that note, Nike is about to become a dividend aristocrat in 2026, after increasing its dividend for 24 consecutive years so far.

After reaching an all-time high of $179.10 per share in November 2021, Nike closed at $44.20 as of April 14th, which is equivalent to a ~75% decline in less than five years. As illustrated in the chart below, this is the largest drawdown in the company's history as a public company, that is since its initial public offering in December 1980. Interestingly enough, Apple (AAPL) completed its IPO just 10 days later, with Tim Cook serving on Nike's board of directors since 2005.

With such a large drawdown, the question is whether this is an opportunity or a trap. My 'Buy' rating certainly gives an indication of my personal views, but I would also like to highlight that Tim Cook and Nike's recently appointed CEO Elliot Hill both disclosed significant share purchases over the last few days. More specifically, Tim Cook purchased 25,000 shares at an average price of $42.43 on April 10th, followed by Mr. Hill's purchase of 23,660 shares at an average price of $42.27 on April 13th. For disclosure purposes, I would also like to mention that I acquired my first Nike shares on April 7th at an average price of $42.58, close enough to the low of $42.09 recorded on the next day. Other directors also purchased shares on the open market over the last few days, including Robert Swan and John Rogers, with 11,781 and 4,000 shares, respectively.

Even though it is not possible to reach any definitive conclusion based on insider transactions alone, the recent $1.0 million purchase by the company's chief executive officer shows some commitment and confidence regarding its turnaround plan.

Financial Overview

During the third quarter of fiscal 2026, Nike reported revenues of $11.3 billion, which were flat on a year-over-year basis, or down by -3% on a constant currency basis. This is already a significant improvement versus the -9% decline in revenues reported during the full year of 2025 and the -11% drop in constant currency disclosed in the fourth quarter of 2025. Using the Excel add-on Wisesheets, I have compiled Nike's financial statements going all the way back to 2007, and they clearly show that fiscal 2025 was one of the most challenging years in the company's history, with one of the largest declines in revenue ever. For those wanting a more in-depth look at the company's historical financial performance, feel free to download the attached Excel file.

With the appointment of Nike's former chief executive officer John Donahoe back in January 2020, the company executed a shift toward a direct-to-consumer strategy, transferring its focus to online sales rather than its long-time wholesale partners like Foot Locker, JD Sports, or Macy's (M), among many others. As a side note, DICK'S Sporting Goods (DKS) completed the $2.5 billion acquisition of Foot Locker back in September 2025.

In the short-term, Nike's online sales boomed, especially with the worldwide lockdowns associated with the COVID-19 pandemic that only added fuel to the fire. By selling directly to its customers without the intermediary of a retailer, the impact on Nike's profitability was direct, with gross margin reaching an all-time high of 45.8% in the fourth quarter of 2021. Almost five years later, Nike reported a gross margin of 40.2%, which is still good, but nowhere near what was delivered over the last few years.

In fact, Nike has been quite aggressive in pursuing promotional activities over the last few quarters, in an attempt to bring its inventory position to healthier levels and to protect its brand value, as discussed in the most recent earnings conference call.
